coredata.dc:title: Enhanced wood-derived photothermal evaporation system by in-situ incorporated lignin carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Chao, Weixiang
Li, Yudong
Sun, Xiaohan
Cao, Guoliang
Wang, Chengyu
Ho, Shih-Hsin
coredata.dc:description: Photothermal evaporation and desalination via renewable solar energy has promising potential to alleviate freshwater scarcity. However, recent reported systems usually lack sufficient environmental compatibility, ecological security, and energy-saving concern. Here, a novel and green photothermal evaporation system with ecological and economic advantages was designed. An evaporation performance of 1.18 kg·m−2 (1.09 kg·m−2·h−1) with up to 79.5% efficiency at one sun illumination (1 kW·m−2) was achieved. More importantly, a series of simulation and numerical modelling was synchronously developed to analyze in-depth the main factors that affect water transportation and evaporation processes. The channel size, temperature distribution, and formed gradient were carefully investigated and discussed. Notably, this system exhibited satisfactory repeatability and stability. In addition, only a few photothermal components are required in this system, which will also bring a significant economic merit. Taken together, this work successfully provides new insights into developing a sustainable photothermal evaporation system with ecologically friendly property and satisfactory performance in practical application. Moreover, it also reveals the corresponding water transportation and photothermal evaporation mechanisms, and maximizes the evaporation efficiency.

head.ce:title: Enhanced wood-derived photothermal evaporation system by in-situ incorporated lignin carbon quantum dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photothermal evaporation and desalination via renewable solar energy has promising potential to alleviate freshwater scarcity. However, recent reported systems usually lack sufficient environmental compatibility, ecological security, and energy-saving concern. Here, a novel and green photothermal evaporation system with ecological and economic advantages was designed. An evaporation performance of 1.18 kg·m−2 (1.09 kg·m−2·h−1) with up to 79.5% efficiency at one sun illumination (1 kW·m−2) was achieved. More importantly, a series of simulation and numerical modelling was synchronously developed to analyze in-depth the main factors that affect water transportation and evaporation processes. The channel size, temperature distribution, and formed gradient were carefully investigated and discussed. Notably, this system exhibited satisfactory repeatability and stability. In addition, only a few photothermal components are required in this system, which will also bring a significant economic merit. Taken together, this work successfully provides new insights into developing a sustainable photothermal evaporation system with ecologically friendly property and satisfactory performance in practical application. Moreover, it also reveals the corresponding water transportation and photothermal evaporation mechanisms, and maximizes the evaporation efficiency.

ce:section.ce:para: Fresh water resources are currently deteriorating due to excessive wastewater discharge and industry/urban usage [1–3]. Seawater desalination is a promising alternative for water scarcity mitigation [4–6]. Compared to various conventional techniques such as multistage flash distillation and reverse osmosis filtration that may require relatively high energy supply with extra infrastructure [7,8], the photothermal evaporation (PE) method exhibits noteworthy advantages in not only saving tremendous energy by efficiently utilizing renewable solar energy, but also minimizing environmental impacts [9–14].

To achieve desired high performance, it is necessary to develop an effective light harvesting system with efficient light absorption ability, less thermal diffusion near the air-liquid interface, and lower-tortuosity channel for water/vapor transportation. Currently, many studies have successfully constructed evaporation systems by introducing different porous substrates, such as metal oxide foams, polymer membranes [15,16], carbon-based foams [17,18] and commercial sponge [19,20], as the transportation matrix and photothermal components for improved solar conversion [8,21–24]. However, the relatively high production cost with high energy consumption and severe environmental impact (e.g. the low biocompatibility and high toxicity) of these materials may largely limit the downstream practical application potential of PE technology. Therefore, developing a PE system exhibiting higher applicability in the actual situations should be further considered.

Wood, as an abundant and sustainable natural product, possesses a hierarchical porous structure with great low-tortuosity channels allowing for water transportation [12,14,25–27]. The anisotropic thermal conduction along and across its growth direction [12,28,29] makes wood a better thermal manager at the air-water interface with decreased thermal diffusion to water. These merits may endow wood with promising potential as a superior green porous substrate in the PE system. Unfortunately, most relevant studies on wood-based PE systems only used wood as the porous matrix decorated with some environmentally-harmful and costly components such as plasmonic metal nanoparticles [30] or semi-conducting materials [31,32], which may contradict the initial intension of making an environmentally-friendly PE system from wood.

To date, due to the excellent solar energy utilization ability, carbon-based decorations such as graphene [19,33–36], carbon nanotubes [37,38], and carbon quantum dots [39,40] have been widely applied to make photothermal-related conversion systems, utilizing their advantages of high solar absorbance, stability, and abundance [41]. However, the limitations in scalability, quality, cost, energy-saving, and environmental friendliness may restrict their practical applications. Hence, fabrication of a novel carbon-based photothermal component with favorable photothermal conversion ability, lower energy consumption, and less environmental impact should be focused on to better meet the real demand. Lignin, one of the main natural polymers [42,43], possesses an aromatic bio-structure that can be easily converted to graphitic carbon through mild catalytic pyrolysis [44–46]. However, natural wood normally contains inherent lignin component in the inner lumen cell walls for structural function. This lignin component exists in an amorphous form, which cannot easily achieve photothermal conversion. Besides, the delignified lignin is also treated as waste in most industrial production. However, its inherent high-defect structure may promote the formation of random carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to sp2 hybrid ordered structure with graphene crystal [21,47–49]. Therefore, the lignin-derived CQDs can not only guarantee favorable compatibility within a wood-based PE system, but also assist in absorbing solar illumination to induce local vibration for enhancing photothermal thermogenesis.

In this work, to efficiently utilize renewable solar energy and minimize environmental impact, a novel and green PE system was developed by incorporating delignified wood (DW) (mainly used for vapor transportation) with lignin-derived carbon quantum dots (LCQDs) (mainly used for photothermal conversion). More importantly, a series of simulations and numerical modelling associated with actual measurements was developed to comprehensively reveal the structure-related mechanisms and dominant effects. Overall, this work successfully demonstrates a green and sustainable PE system with high environmental compatibility and satisfactory evaporation efficiency. It also clearly elucidates the water transportation and photothermal conversion mechanisms of the as-prepared PE system. Knowledge obtained from this work will be useful in assessing the utilization of renewable solar energy and wood-based resources, and enhancing the understanding of wood-based PE mechanisms.

Balsa wood (Ochroma Lagopus, Balsa of the kapok family) were used in this work and the dimensions of samples were set as 10 × 10 × 2.5 (mm). Sodium chlorite (NaClO2, 80%), wood-extracted lignin powders (85%), anhydrous sodium acetate (CH3COONa, 99%), glacial acetic acid (HAc, 99.5%) and ethanediamine (EDA, 99%) were purchased from Macklin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Hydrogen peroxide solution (H2O2, ω = 30%) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Ethyl alcohol (99.7%) was purchased from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Dialysis tube was purchased from Viskase America (Lombard, USA) with molecular retention for 3000 Dalton. All chemicals were used as received without any purification.

DW was obtained through delignification in a solution containing ac. 2 wt% NaClO2 in buffer solution with approximate pH value of 4.6. The wood was immersed into the solution and boiled for 6 h until the wood turned completely white. The obtained DW was rinsed in pure water thrice to remove residual chemicals and bleached in ac. 5 wt% H2O2 solution. The bleached DW was rinsed with pure water and freeze-dried for 36 h to obtain the final sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrothermal fabrication of lignin-derived carbon quantum dots (LCQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: LCQDs were fabricated through the hydrothermal method [45]. First, 0.1 g lignin was dispersed in 9.4 mL pure water with subsequent ultrasonic treatment for 5 min. Then, a small amount of ethanediamine (EDA) (600 μL) was added into the dispersion with magnetic stirring for thorough mixing. The mixture was transferred to an autoclave with a para-polystyrene (PPL) container. After reaction for 12 h at 180 °C, the reaction mixture was filtered through a 0.22 μm microfiltration hydrophilic membrane. The filtrate liquor was dialyzed against pure water for one week. Finally, the dialyzed filtrate liquor was placed in an open evaporating dish and dried in an 80 °C oven to obtain the LCQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drop-coating for coating DW with LCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: A uniform and stable LCQDs suspension was obtained via re-dispersing 30 mg LCQDs in 1 mL ethyl alcohol by ultra-sonication. The suspension was then dropped onto the DW. The decorated DW was dried at 60 °C for 20 min to obtain the DW coated with LCQDs (LCQDs-DW).

The morphologies were observed by scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi TM3030, Japan) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEM-2100, Japan). Specific surface area and porosity tester (JW-BK132F, JWGB, China) was used to collect the specific surface area and pore size distributions. Chemical composition and crystal variation were determined by energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopic detector, X-ray diffractometer (XRD, X' Pert 3 Powedr, PANalytical, Netherlands), X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS, THERMO, USA), Raman spectrometer (HORIBA, LabRAM HR Evolution, Japan), Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (FT-IR, Nicolet is50, Thermo fisher, USA). Thermal stability was investigated by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA, Q50, Ta, USA). Surface wettability of as-prepared samples were determined by an OCA20 Contact angle measurement system (Data-physics, Germany). The UV–visible-NIR spectrophotometer (UV-3600, Shimadzu, Japan) was used to measure the light absorption. An inductively coupled plasma source mass spectrometer (ICP-MS, Agilent 7800ce, USA) was used to detect the ions concentration before and after desalination. Details of characterization conditions, evaporation measurement, parameters calculation, controlled experiments setup, repeatability and stability measurement were provided in Supplementary information. Modelling parameters, boundary conditions and calculation methods of computational fluid dynamics (CFD) were also supplemented in Supplementary information.

The prepared samples were labelled as follows: pure water for complete phase of simulated seawater (the preparation is illustrated in the Supplementary information), DW for delignified wood, LCQDs for lignin-derived carbon quantum dots, LCQDs-DW for DW decorated with LCQDs, wood/water for wood floating over evaporated water, DW/water for DW floating over evaporated water, and LCQDs-DW/water for LCQDs-DW floating over evaporated water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of chemical composition and morphology
ce:section.ce:para: Inherent wood channels with low-tortuosity have been reported for multi component transfer through solar energy motivated transpiration [13]. The compatibility of wood with other functional components provides its favorable decoration potential [39] (Fig. 1 ). In this work, the biomorphic structure of wood was reserved after delignification, along with decoration of the wood-based LCQDs (Fig. 1a-c). Benefitting from the aromatic lattice structure of LCQDs, the LCQDs-DW may be able to induce photothermal conversion and may be useful for the PE system (Fig. 1d-e) [39]. LCQDs also exhibited favorable affinity and combination with DW since both components were derived from wood, which guarantees a favorable compatibility in the wood-based PE system. The channels of DW after decoration by LCQDs were indeed able to function as a solar light absorber. Due to the inherited structure from natural wood, the tortuosity in both DW and LCQDs-DW was low, exhibiting a straight lumen channel structure. As shown in Fig. 1d, because of the inner lumen structure, there should be certain reflection and refraction of solar illumination when the light falls on the surface and channels of samples, which form the light-trapping phenomenon. This phenomenon can favor the general solar light absorption as more solar illumination is absorbed and functions within the inner structure of samples. In addition, the LCQDs decoration did not significantly affect the structure of DW. This was because this PE system has a suitable structure with enough inner hierarchical porous functional sites and serves as a medium for trapping the incident light in the channels. Moreover, the LCQDs can also be decorated around the inner region of DW, which can facilitate photothermal thermogenesis to promote the overall evaporation.

Fig. 2 a-c shows the macro-morphology of wood, DW, and LCQDs-DW, respectively. Due to removal of chromophoric groups from the wood, the DW turned completely white. After decoration with LCQDs, the LCQDs-DW featured a deep-brown color under natural light indicating the existence of LCQDs. This suggests that LCQDs were successfully incorporated with the DW. In this study, due to the mutual affinity between dispersed LCQDs in a concentrated suspension, partial LCQDs in precursor suspension gradually self-formed the cluster state before decoration. In addition, because the used LCQDs were obtained from hydrothermal self-assembly process, many oxygen-related functional groups were introduced into the structure of LCQDs in both plane and lateral regions. Thus, with the increase in concentration of dispersed suspension, LCQDs combined mutually to offset the carried charges to the stabilized state, and thus formed the cluster state in the LCQDs-DW.

It has been reported that the porous channel structure may provide sufficient space for oriented steam transport [9,12]. Fortunately, as seen from Fig. 2d-f, the delignification and LCQDs decoration treatment did not affect the size of the channels, because the pores at the cross sections did not vary remarkably. All pore sizes were between 80 and 100 μm before and after treatment. The channel structure along the Z direction was reserved, which is beneficial for steam transportation (Fig. 2g-i). Accordingly, these three samples also exhibited similar adsorption-desorption curves and pore distribution (Fig. S1), and similar XRD patterns (Fig. S2), which is in agreement with the notion that using LCQDs as nano-scale dopants does not affect the original structure and properties of wood. Specifically, the delignification and LCQDs decoration mainly affect the situation of the tiny pores in the side lumen walls of wood. The main porous structure with straight channels did not change remarkably after these treatments, as confirmed by the SEM images, which guarantees the original structure of wood for efficient seawater adsorption and directional evaporation. Additionally, in this study, we selected soft wood species to fabricate samples to provide sufficient channels for steam flow. This soft wood may not be able to suffer bending and result in cracks. Fortunately, as shown in Fig. S3, due to the natural inherited straight channel structure and the strong connection between mutual basic cellulose components, the cracked sample still maintained the original structure allowing efficient evaporation, which can guarantee its practical application.

Fig. 2j characterizes the lattice structure of LCQDs with spacing estimated to be 0.22 nm. This corresponds to the lattice spacing of graphene (100) crystal plane and indicates the graphite sp2 carbon clusters of LCQDs. Such structure can achieve efficient lattice vibration with incident light for photothermal thermogenesis [21,50,51]. When the solar illumination falls into the lattice structure, there would be lattice vibration with thermal energy generation. Besides, partial solar energy would also trigger an electron transition from different energy levels. During the process, the energy loss would also convert to thermal energy. All the generated thermal energy would function as photothermal thermogenesis for evaporation. XRD and Raman spectra were also obtained to elucidate the crystalline structure of the LCQDs. As shown in Fig. 2k, a narrow diffraction peak (002) of LCQDs with interlayer spacing of 3.79 Å corresponds to bulk graphite at 23.4°. Inversely, a broad peak in lignin at 20.3° was assigned to interlayer spacing of 4.38 Å, confirming its distinct amorphous structure. Raman spectrum of the LCQDs further demonstrated the formation of graphitization with an ID/IG ratio of 1.498 (Fig. 2l). In addition, no obvious graphite peaks were observed in lignin, indicating its more polymer-like structure. The pure LCQDs also exhibited certain photo-response, as shown in Fig. S4. The LCQDs exhibited a signal attenuation with increasing excitation wavelength, and a glaucous response under UV light (365 nm) illumination. These results suggest that the presence of defect density in LCQDs structure is more similar to graphite than lignin.

EDX mapping indicated the uniform distribution of N element in LCQDs-DW (Fig. S5). A significant increase in N content in LCQDs-DW illustrated that the LCQDs were successfully decorated onto the surface of DW (Fig. 2m-n). Moreover, XPS (Fig. 2o-v) and FT-IR (Fig. S6) spectra also suggested the effective incorporation between LCQDs and DW since a N 1s peak was observed in both LCQDs and LCQDs-DW. Moreover, N–H (400.17 eV), C–N (403.2 eV), and N–O (406.45 eV) bonds were observed in LCQDs-DW. N–O stretching was recorded around 1510 cm−1. In contrast, N element was not detected in lignin, wood or DW since N element was introduced by nitrogen-atom doping (Fig. S7). As a result, the LCQDs-DW can achieve efficient photothermal ability due to the successful decoration by LCQDs. Generally, due to the same resources from wood, the combination and compatibility between LCQDs and DW is favorable. Moreover, after hydro-thermal fabrication, the LCQDs formed some amounts of oxygen-related groups, and the delignification of DW exposed more cellulose-based surface with more hydroxy groups. These functional groups can form certain strong chemical bonds such as hydrogen bonds, which can enhance the chemical stability and efficient combination. The strong binding combination should be able to guarantee the chemical stability against the effects of photothermal thermogenesis and water/steam flow.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simulation of water transportation process by LCQDs-DW porous matrix adsorption
ce:section.ce:para: Before photothermal-induced evaporation, water is initially adsorbed into the channels of the porous substrate to form the air-water interface. However, the ability to monitor the detailed dynamics of the water transportation process is still challenging. Thus, simulations associated with actual experiments were conducted to comprehensively reveal the corresponding mechanism by investigating the dominant effects. Detailed information of the calculation method and the simulation analysis is provided in the Supplementary information.

Compared with wood, DW and LCQDs-DW showed much faster water adsorption efficiency due to their hydrophilic improvements (Fig. 3 a-c). Benefiting from this property, the LCQDs-DW formed a satisfactory air-water interface with continuously holding water in the channels without a sink and swelling (Fig. 3d), which is essential for enhancing the efficiency of the PE system. The interface can result from the unoccupied space with water in channels. As shown in Fig. S8, due to the inherent lignin component over the surface of wood, which was water-resistant to some extent, the water adsorption within the channels was actually lower than the situation in DW and LCQDs-DW. Besides, DW and LCQDs-DW could still form sufficient air-water interface as floating over water. The capillary force also played a vital role in adsorption. In theory, the capillary force is highly related with the surface tension, which is affected by the size of adsorbed medium. Herein, a series of simulations was introduced to disclose the effect of these factors on the water adsorption of LCQDs-DW.

Fig. 3e-f demonstrates the potential adsorbed height with increasing time and pore size. The height gradually increased with an increase in pore size until it reached 80 μm diameter, which indicated that the LCQDs-DW with 80–100 µm pore size was more preferable for creating a higher air-water interface. A higher air-water interface can absorb more solar energy due to the larger illumination area. In addition, the air-water interface ratio dramatically increased with increase in pore size from 10 μm to 80 μm, suggesting that the larger pore size could form a more efficient air-water interface for enhancing contact opportunities with light. This pore size distribution was also in accordance with the LCQDs-DW in SEM images (Fig. 2). A further increase in pore size from 80 μm to 100 μm resulted in nearly no difference in air-water interface ratio, which may suggest that the surface tension at the air-water interface began to level off when the pore size exceeded a critical value.

With the induced photothermal thermogenesis and the subsequent thermal conducting, the temperature at air-water interface increased continuously. The increased temperature thus induced the interfacial water evaporation below the boiling point. The evaporation process can be described with coupled classical Laplace equation and Kelvin equation as follows:

Thus, to achieve the equation balance, the parameter relationship between r and σ can be explained as follows. The larger curvature radius of the channels at air-water interface against lower surface tension due to the negative correlation in the equation, and the curvature radius of the channels at air-water interface were negatively related with the radius size of the channels in Eq. (2). Thus, the capillary force function at the air-water interface should be highly related with the surface tension, which was affected by the size of adsorbed medium. Adsorption at the hierarchical pore size in height, volume, and pressure field can also bring more visualized evidence to better explain the water evaporation process (Figs. S9 and 10).

From the viewpoint of transportation flow pattern, the water adsorption flow in channels can involve the inlet region and the inner-channel region (Fig. S11). A turn shape appeared at the inlet near the bottom of the channel, which could be caused by the consumption of energy provided by capillary-force. The turn exhibited the macro velocity distribution approaching inlet (Fig. 3g), which resulted in different velocities against varied diameters. As demonstrated in Fig. 3h, the rising speed for adsorption was reduced upon increasing the diameter, which means less energy was consumed at the inlet and higher utilization of structure-related energy was exhibited. This phenomenon was also verified by the shearing rate at the inlet region (Fig. S12). More importantly, less energy loss can lead to higher adsorption and subsequently larger air-water interface in order to accept more solar illumination. As a result, through the series simulation, the structure of LCQDs-DW, especially in the channel size distribution, is suitable for promoting the formation of the air-water evaporation interface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Clarification of enhanced photothermal evaporation performance by LCQDs-DW
ce:section.ce:para: In addition to having the low-tortuosity and photothermal component, thermal management ability is also vital for efficient water evaporation. As illustrated in Fig. 4 a, the LCQDs-DW exhibited similar thermal conductivity to wood and DW, suggesting that the nano-scale of LCQDs decorated on DW had no function in the whole conduction network. Thus, the inherited anisotropic property of LCQDs-DW can promote heat concentration at the air-water interface, whereas the anisotropic thermal conductivity of LCQDs-DW can promote the evaporation efficiency through increasing heat concentration. As thermal conduction can be enhanced along the direction parallel to channels and reduced along the direction perpendicular to channels, more photothermal thermogenesis can conduct downward from the illumination surface to inner air-water interfaces in channels while less heat loss can occur within the channels. To illustrate the light absorption of LCQDs-DW, UV–Vis light-absorption spectra were obtained (Fig. 4b). DW exhibited nearly no light absorption due to lack of chromophoric groups. In contrast, benefiting from the graphite structure, LCQDs displayed a favorable selective absorption. Notably, even after the LCQDs were decorated onto DW, the light absorption ability of LCQDs-DW was as good as the LCQDs, indicating that the favorable photothermal effect of LCQDs-DW would be conducive to solar-induced thermogenesis.

Benefiting from the aforementioned properties, LCQDs-DW exhibited a much higher temperature rise rate (ΔT) than wood and DW, which approached nearly 30 °C within 10 min (Fig. 4c). This clearly demonstrates that the light absorption of DW was largely inhibited due to a lack of chromophoric groups, indicating that the LCQDs decorated on the DW surface is a superior photothermal converter. In contrast, the lignin-based chromophoric group existing in wood can contribute to increasing the light absorption, but the amorphous structure of lignin and other substances beyond lignin may reduce both the light absorption and photothermal conversion to a certain degree. Thus, LCQDs-DW could be a potential candidate for solar-induced thermogenesis.

To reduce the energy loss, in this study, a thermal-protective coating was applied around the evaporation region under illumination to minimize the potential heat loss, which was monitored by specific measurement devices (see Supplementary information). As the evaporated region was coated by heat-insulating wool, the heat loss was largely reduced and efficient freshwater collection was then achieved. A homemade device to evaluate the practical performance of LCQDs-DW PE system is illustrated in Fig. 4d. Fig. 4e exhibits the infrared thermal images of LCQDs-DW/water under one sun illumination, and the region with the dashed rectangle represents the LCQDs-DW that performed the evaporation. The concentrated thermal energy could thus heat the air–water interface to trigger the evaporation process. As demonstrated in Fig. 4f, when the initial temperature was set as 20 °C, the temperature of LCQDs-DW/water reached ~31 °C in 20 min and remained at a higher value compared to pure water (~23 °C in 20 min), wood/water (~26 °C in 20 min) and DW/water (~25 °C in 20 min). LCQDs-DW exhibited enhanced evaporation mass change (1.18 kg·m−2 (1.09 kg·m−2·h−1)) compared to pure water (0.24 kg·m−2), wood/water (0.81 kg·m−2) and DW/water (0.71 kg·m−2) (Fig. 4g). This enhanced evaporation performance can be beneficial for harvesting solar energy in outdoor practical application due to the real-time changed weather condition. Practical measurement for seawater under actual sun irradiation was also conducted in this study, as shown in Fig. S13. Due to the inevitable energy loss, the evaporation mass change was slightly lower than the measured value in laboratory conditions with controlled room temperature and less energy loss.

To quantitatively assess the performance of LCQDs-DW in PE system, parameters including maximum temperature, overall temperature rise, and evaporation mass change were defined and measured. The LCQDs-DW showed the best performance among pure water, wood, and DW (Fig. S14). In addition, the evaporation efficiency was also obtained according to the calculation provided in the Supplementary information. As illustrated in Fig. 4h, the highest evaporation efficiency was achieved at 79.5% in LCQDs-DW, demonstrating its superior application potential. In contrast, the evaporation efficiency of water, wood, and DW only reached 16.2%, 54.2%, and 47.4%, respectively, which may result from their insufficient thermal conduction and low photothermal conversion. Furthermore, to evaluate the applicability of LCQDs-DW in desalination, investigation of ion selectivity was also performed. Table 1 displays the main positive ions (i.e. Na+, Mg2+, K+, Ca2+, Ba2+, and B3+) that existed in simulated seawater and were nearly 100% removed using LCQDs-DW. Moreover, the concentrations of these main ions after desalination perfectly meet the international standard of drinking water provided by the World Health Organization (WHO). This clearly illustrates that LCQDs-DW can offer a highly-efficient selective evaporation process by continuously evaporating out the freshwater components in the system, making LCQDs-DW potential promising for freshwater production.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the evaporation-related dominant factors of LCQDs-DW via numerical modelling
ce:section.ce:para: To better understand the role of LCQDs-DW for enhancing photothermal evaporation, computational fluid dynamics (CFD) modelling was developed. The details of the calculation method, modelling parameter, and boundary condition are provided in the Supplementary information.

Before simulation, the temperature distribution along the Z direction through LCQDs-DW down to water affected the evaporation was proposed. As shown in Fig. 5 a and b, the temperature distribution (calculated from the average in Fig. 5c) from LCQDs-DW/water to water tank was mainly separated into four regions including 1-air to upper surface of the LCQDs-DW, 2-upper to lower surface of the LCQDs-DW, 3-air-water interface, and 4-water tank. Specifically, most of the thermal energy was concentrated near the air-water interface. The upper to lower surface of the LCQDs-DW had a temperature gradient, suggesting a thermal conduction towards the air-water interface via LCQDs-DW. Despite the thermal transmission from air-water interface to water tank dynamically existed. Luckily, in this study, it did not significantly affect the overall evaporation efficiency because most of the photothermal thermogenesis was actually concentrated at the air-water interface, which is the main region conducting photothermal evaporation. Moreover, as the air-water interface was formed within the LCQDs-DW, no direct contact with bulk water occurred, which would largely alleviate the thermal energy loss benefiting from its anisotropic thermal conductivity. Thus, although certain amount of thermal energy would conduct to water tank, this study demonstrates that most photothermal thermogenesis was concentrated at air-water interface to efficiently trigger water evaporation. Both the concentrated thermal energy and the efficient downward conduction thus triggered the water evaporation.

In general, the temperature distribution and gradient are vital factors for enhancing water evaporation efficiency. As illustrated in Fig. 5d, the simulated temperature variation between the upper surface of LCQDs-DW and the air-water interface was revealed. The temperature of these two interfaces increased with increasing light exposure time until it reached a steady stage. More importantly, a temperature gradient that existed in the steady stage approached the air-water interface region (assumed as area approaching the upper surface of LCQDs-DW) (Fig. 5e). This temperature gradient triggered the formation of downward thermal conduction to the air-water interface through LCQDs-DW, which is in an agreement with the aforementioned prediction. The final transient temperature distribution also verified the downward tendency of thermal conduction (Fig. 5f). Taken together, these simulations clearly disclose the irreplaceability of temperature distribution and gradient in the water evaporation process.

Further investigation on the thermal-coupled steam flow was also conducted, and the corresponding modelling is shown in Fig. S15. Due to the low velocity of evaporation (Fig. 5g), the flow-in channel for both water and vapor steam can be regarded as the laminar flow, which can reduce the loss of fluid kinetic energy and promote its transportation. Flow in channels is mostly divided into two flow types according to the dynamic pattern. The turbulence flow normally exhibits fast velocity and mixed flow. In this study, most of the fluid kinetic energy in the flow was consumed until the flow was terminated. In contrast, the laminar flow usually conducts directionally with relatively slow velocity and consumes less fluid kinetic energy, resulting in higher potential flow transportation. Moreover, the major motivation in LCQDs-DW system for seawater transportation is provided by capillary force, demonstrating that only few motivations can be further efficiently utilized without considerable energy loss. Therefore, these findings indicate that the laminar flow can contribute to reducing the loss of fluid kinetic along with promoting the transportation.

Additionally, due to the different thermal conduction ability between air and water, the conduction can be regarded as multi-layered (Fig. S16). The temperature at the middle of the air-water interface can be estimated through the quasi-static hypothesis (the heat fluxes through the air field boundary, the air-water interface, and the water field boundary reached a steady situation and were regarded as the same value). As shown in Fig. 5h, the steam flow-in channel can also contribute to the developing evaporation process, which is consistent with the thermal-related simulations. In addition, the evolution of water evaporation exhibited in Fig. 5i illustrated that the higher temperature constantly appears at the upper surface of LCQDs-DW thermal concentrated area, while simultaneously conducting the water adsorption until saturation. Eventually, the steam flow would be generated along the channel motivated by the temperature gradient. These simulations indicate the significance of temperature distribution, thermal conduction, and flow situation of PE process, which can be helpful for further improving the PE system by carefully considering these aspects.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the practical applicability of LCQDs-DW
ce:section.ce:para: To meet the practical demand, it is necessary to examine the stability of solar-induced evaporation. Fig. S17 exhibits the infrared thermal images of LCQDs-DW/water repeated for two to four cycles. Note that the main performance including temperature rise, evaporation mass change, and several main parameters defined did not vary distinctively (Fig. 6 a-c). Fig. 6d also shows that there was only a slight fluctuation in evaporation efficiency through the five cycles. The structural stability of LCQDs-DW after cycles is vital for the evaporation system in practical application. As shown in Fig. S18, this study also compared the SEM images of LCQDs-DW after cycles and no obvious changes occurred (the sample was obtained through a secondary freeze drying to remove water and maintain structure; otherwise, the natural drying would cause structural shrinkage), which demonstrates the structural stability of LCQDs-DW through cyclic usage. As shown in Fig. S19, the EDX mapping of LCQDs-DW after cyclic evaporation also showed a similar composed elements distribution and proportion compared the sample before evaporation, which confirmed the chemical stability for LCQDs-DW against the effects by photothermal thermogenesis and water/steam flow.

Moreover, since evaporation ability is highly related to the amount of thermal energy, TGA was also conducted to determine the thermal stability. As shown in Fig. 6e-f, ignoring the partial mass degradation of samples below 100 °C correlated to inherent water evaporation of samples, the mass degradation curve was measured under the atmosphere to simulate the actual situation, and no degradation occurred under the upper limit temperature (200 °C). These results demonstrate that the LCQDs-DW possess excellent stability in both repeatability and thermal property, largely enhancing its practical viability.

To optimize the performance of PE system, LCQDs-DW were decorated with hierarchical concentrations of LCQDs suspension. Fig. S20 exhibits the corresponding infrared thermal images of LCQDs-DW/water. As illustrated in Fig. 7 a-b and Fig. S21, in addition to the evaporation mass change increased to 1.18 kg·m−2, no distinct variation was found with increasing the LCQDs concentration. The evaporation efficiency (Fig. 7c) also increased as increasing LCQD photothermal sites were introduced into the LCQDs-DW, which also confirmed the increasing solar absorption, as illustrated in Fig. S22. The similar evaporation performance that resulted from the nanoscale decoration of LCQDs onto DW showed no obvious negative influence that appeared in the DW structure for both adsorption-desorption and pore sizes distribution (Fig. S23). These results indicate that only a small amount of LCQDs decoration is sufficient to effectively trigger the PE system, which indicates great economic merit in practical fabrication.

Due to the world’s wide latitude distribution, real solar illumination power density in different areas and across different seasons will also result in varying PE performance. Fig. S24 exhibits the corresponding infrared thermal images of LCQDs-DW/water under hierarchical solar illumination. As illustrated in Fig. 7d-e and Fig. S25, the temperature rise, evaporation mass change, and other main parameters improved with an increase in solar power. However, the evaporation efficiency had a decreasing trend with increasing solar power (Fig. 7f). This phenomenon can be explained by the insufficient utilization of solar illumination, which may result from the selective light absorption property of LCQDs-DW mainly for visible-light absorption.

The comparison of performance, cost, scalability, and environmental adverse effects between this work and other relevant research is shown in Table S1 [8,29,30,52–56]. Considering its excellent photothermal conversion ability and evaporation performance, LCQDs-DW can function as an efficient and practical steam generation device. Notably, the compositions of LCQDs-DW were all derived from natural wood with trace amount of additional chemicals, which can reduce the energy consumption during fabrication and post disposal treatment to better meet the real demands of ecological security and practical application.

This work provides an environmentally friendly and efficient solar-driven photothermal evaporation system to help alleviate the world’s freshwater resource crisis. The system was fabricated via LCQDs-DW that were all derived from natural wood, which can efficiently utilize the renewable solar energy while minimizing the ecological effects. The as-prepared PE system achieved excellent performance by exhibiting over 10 °C thermogenesis rise and 1.18 kg·m−2 (1.09 kg·m−2·h−1) evaporation mass change with photothermal conversion efficiency up to 79.5% under one sun illumination. Also, a better performance of 11.10 kg·m−2 evaporation mass change was achieved under 10 sun illumination. Notably, a series of simulations and numerical modelling associated with actual measurements was developed in order to reveal the corresponding mechanisms of both water transportation and photothermal evaporation flow, and completely explain several dominant effects on water transportation and evaporation process. The channel size, temperature distribution, and formed temperature gradient were identified as the main factors that affect the processes. Moreover, the system durability, ion removal efficiency, and process optimization were also monitored to better meet the practical demand. Taken together, we successfully fabricated a PE system, which can not only achieve the goal of full utilization of wood resources, but also provide satisfactory performance of practical water evaporation. Overall, we believe this work offers new insights into wood-derived PE system preparation, mechanistic explanation, and practical application potential, which may be helpful in the development of practical-scaled new desalination and water evaporation strategies.
